Maladaptive Strategies.value: deliberately try to leave my body or blank out
I distract myself with something else
I try to erase the memory of the event

Threat.value: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
I frequently look behind me to check if someone is following me
I am unable to relax even when I know I am in a safe place

Memory.value: My memories of the event feel more real and vivid than memories of ordinary things
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.
When I remember the event I feel overwhelming grief

Negative Appraisals.value: Nothing good can happen to me anymore
The people responsible have faced no consequences

vignette:
Marianne, a 79-year-old North American judge who lives alone, sought help because she remains on edge despite a long professional life in which she is expected to appear composed and decisive. She was raped years ago, at a moment when she was physically overpowered and unable to get away. She recalls the attacker closing in, the loss of control, and the helplessness of not being able to stop what was happening. That experience has made the route from court to her car, public corridors, and parking areas especially fraught for her, and in those settings she becomes acutely aware of who is behind her and whether she can relax long enough to feel safe.

When Marianne speaks about the assault, she does not describe it as distant. She says it comes back “as if it is happening again,” with a sharp, detailed quality that feels more immediate than ordinary memories. At times she stops mid-sentence, looks away, and becomes tearful, saying that when she remembers it she is flooded with grief. She also reports trying to push the memory out of mind altogether, as though she could erase it if she worked hard enough. Her sense that nothing good can happen to her anymore and that she has no future keeps the memory active and close.

Her central belief is bleak and unyielding: “Nothing good can happen to me anymore. I have no future. The people responsible got away with it.” She speaks with the certainty of someone who feels the assault confirmed that there is no real justice and no protected life waiting ahead. The strain of feeling constantly unsafe leaves her with the conviction that her future is empty and that the people responsible faced no consequences.

Marianne stays watchful in a way that is hard to switch off. She frequently looks over her shoulder in hallways and on the way from chambers to the parking lot, and she notices her body tightening whenever something reminds her of the assault. Even when she is at home and knows she is physically safe, she says she cannot fully settle. That constant sense of danger leads her to deliberately try to leave her body or go blank, and she distracts herself with whatever task is in front of her in order to get through the moment. Those strategies make the memory of the assault more vivid and more immediate.

Her thinking and her threat state feed one another in a grim loop. The sense that nothing good awaits her, that she has no future, and that no one has been held accountable keeps her scanning for danger and bracing for it. Her alertness keeps the assault feeling present, and that in turn strengthens the same hopeless conclusions. In her work as a judge, this has become most visible in the ordinary movement of her day: she can still preside in court, but she is preoccupied with exits, corridors, and the walk back to her car, and she often leaves with less composure than she shows on the bench.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
Marianne, a 79-year-old North American judge who lives alone, sought help because she remains on edge despite a long professional life in which she is expected to appear composed and decisive. She was raped years ago, at a moment when she was physically overpowered and unable to get away. She recalls the attacker closing in, the loss of control, and the helplessness of not being able to stop what was happening. That experience has left her especially rattled in the places where she now has to move through public corridors, parking areas, and the route between court and her car; in those settings she becomes acutely aware of who is behind her and whether she can relax long enough to feel safe.

When Marianne speaks about the assault, she does not describe it as distant. She says it comes back “as if it is happening again,” with a sharp, detailed quality that feels more immediate than ordinary memories. At times she stops mid-sentence, looks away, and becomes tearful, saying that when she remembers it she is flooded with grief. She also reports trying to push the memory out of mind altogether, as though she could erase it if she worked hard enough.

Her central belief is bleak and unyielding: “Nothing good can happen to me anymore. I have no future. The people responsible got away with it.” She speaks with the certainty of someone who feels the assault confirmed that there is no real justice and no protected life waiting ahead.

Marianne stays watchful in a way that is hard to switch off. She frequently looks over her shoulder in hallways and on the way from chambers to the parking lot, and she notices her body tightening whenever something reminds her of the assault. Even when she is at home and knows she is physically safe, she says she cannot fully settle. At other times she deliberately tries to leave her body or go blank, and she distracts herself with whatever task is in front of her in order to get through the moment.

Her thinking and her threat state feed one another in a grim loop. The sense that nothing good awaits her, that she has no future, and that no one has been held accountable keeps her scanning for danger and bracing for it. The persistent checking and inability to relax then make the assault feel more present and more vivid, which strengthens the sense of ongoing threat and keeps her returning to the same hopeless conclusions. In her work as a judge, this has become most visible in the ordinary movement of her day: she can still preside in court, but she is preoccupied with exits, corridors, and the walk back to her car, and she often leaves with less composure than she shows on the bench.

violations.explanation: The vignette states negative beliefs and threat states in nearby sentences, but it does not contain a single sentence with explicit causal language linking negative appraisals to threat.
violations.quote: Her central belief is bleak and unyielding: “Nothing good can happen to me anymore. I have no future. The people responsible got away with it.” She speaks with the certainty of someone who feels the assault confirmed that there is no real justice and no protected life waiting ahead.

violations.explanation: The vignette describes negative beliefs and intrusive memory, but no single sentence explicitly says the negative appraisals cause the memory to return or intensify.
violations.quote: When Marianne speaks about the assault, she does not describe it as distant. She says it comes back “as if it is happening again,” with a sharp, detailed quality that feels more immediate than ordinary memories.

violations.explanation: Although threat and coping behaviors are both described, the vignette does not provide one sentence with explicit causal language showing threat leading to maladaptive strategies.
violations.quote: Her thinking and her threat state feed one another in a grim loop. The sense that nothing good awaits her, that she has no future, and that no one has been held accountable keeps her scanning for danger and bracing for it.

violations.explanation: The vignette notes threat and memory experiences, but it does not contain a single sentence with explicit causal wording that threat produces or intensifies memory.
violations.quote: The persistent checking and inability to relax then make the assault feel more present and more vivid, which strengthens the sense of ongoing threat and keeps her returning to the same hopeless conclusions.

violations.explanation: The vignette presents threat and negative appraisals as part of a loop, but it does not explicitly state in one sentence that threat causes the negative beliefs.
violations.quote: Her thinking and her threat state feed one another in a grim loop. The persistent checking and inability to relax then make the assault feel more present and more vivid, which strengthens the sense of ongoing threat and keeps her returning to the same hopeless conclusions.

violations.explanation: The vignette mentions checking, avoiding, and dissociative coping, but no single sentence explicitly states that these maladaptive strategies cause the memory to become more present or vivid.
violations.quote: At other times she deliberately tries to leave her body or go blank, and she distracts herself with whatever task is in front of her in order to get through the moment.

- Negative Appraisals → Threat: The vignette states negative beliefs and threat states in nearby sentences, but it does not contain a single sentence with explicit causal language linking negative appraisals to threat.
- Negative Appraisals → Memory: The vignette describes negative beliefs and intrusive memory, but no single sentence explicitly says the negative appraisals cause the memory to return or intensify.
- Threat → Maladaptive Strategies: Although threat and coping behaviors are both described, the vignette does not provide one sentence with explicit causal language showing threat leading to maladaptive strategies.
- Threat → Memory: The vignette notes threat and memory experiences, but it does not contain a single sentence with explicit causal wording that threat produces or intensifies memory.
- Threat → Negative Appraisals: The vignette presents threat and negative appraisals as part of a loop, but it does not explicitly state in one sentence that threat causes the negative beliefs.
- Maladaptive Strategies → Memory: The vignette mentions checking, avoiding, and dissociative coping, but no single sentence explicitly states that these maladaptive strategies cause the memory to become more present or vivid.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
Marianne, a 79-year-old North American judge who lives alone, sought help because she remains on edge despite a long professional life in which she is expected to appear composed and decisive. She was raped years ago, at a moment when she was physically overpowered and unable to get away. She recalls the attacker closing in, the loss of control, and the helplessness of not being able to stop what was happening. That experience has made the route from court to her car, public corridors, and parking areas especially fraught for her, and in those settings she becomes acutely aware of who is behind her and whether she can relax long enough to feel safe.

When Marianne speaks about the assault, she does not describe it as distant. She says it comes back “as if it is happening again,” with a sharp, detailed quality that feels more immediate than ordinary memories. At times she stops mid-sentence, looks away, and becomes tearful, saying that when she remembers it she is flooded with grief. She also reports trying to push the memory out of mind altogether, as though she could erase it if she worked hard enough. Her sense that nothing good can happen to her anymore and that she has no future makes the memory return with even greater force.

Her central belief is bleak and unyielding: “Nothing good can happen to me anymore. I have no future. The people responsible got away with it.” She speaks with the certainty of someone who feels the assault confirmed that there is no real justice and no protected life waiting ahead. Her sense that she has no future and that no one has been held accountable keeps her braced for danger.

Marianne stays watchful in a way that is hard to switch off. She frequently looks over her shoulder in hallways and on the way from chambers to the parking lot, and she notices her body tightening whenever something reminds her of the assault. Even when she is at home and knows she is physically safe, she says she cannot fully settle. That constant sense of danger leads her to deliberately try to leave her body or go blank, and she distracts herself with whatever task is in front of her in order to get through the moment. Those strategies keep the memory of the assault vivid and close to the surface.

Her thinking and her threat state feed one another in a grim loop. The sense that nothing good awaits her, that she has no future, and that no one has been held accountable keeps her scanning for danger and bracing for it. Her alertness makes the assault feel more immediate, which strengthens the same hopeless conclusions. In her work as a judge, this has become most visible in the ordinary movement of her day: she can still preside in court, but she is preoccupied with exits, corridors, and the walk back to her car, and she often leaves with less composure than she shows on the bench.
